elements.content:
Where JavaScript Runs

- Browser for web pages
- Node.js on servers
- Runtime executes your code
- Built-in APIs come from the environment

Single Thread, One Stack

- One task runs at a time
- Function calls push onto the stack
- Returns pop back off
- The top frame is running now

Async Work Leaves the Stack

- Timers and network calls run outside the engine
- Finished callbacks wait in a queue
- The event loop moves queued work back when the stack is empty

Predict the Output

1. start
2. end
3. promise
4. timeout

Synchronous logs run first. Promise callbacks beat timer callbacks.

Trace the Sample

- `start` runs on the stack
- `setTimeout` registers with Web APIs
- the Promise queues a microtask
- `end` finishes the main script
- microtask runs before timer callback

Environment Changes the APIs

- Browser exposes Web APIs
- Node.js uses libuv for I/O
- The event loop concept stays
- Task timing depends on the runtime

JavaScript in 90 Seconds

- Runs in the browser or Node.js
- Uses one call stack
- Async work waits in queues
- The event loop connects everything

Trace a snippet, then predict the order.
